content.problemStatement:
Now we will implement the functionality to automatically promote a user to **"Moderator"** status when they accumulate 10 or more upvotes across all of their comments.

After this task, a new Moderator model will track which users have special privileges.
- A Django signal will be implemented that listens for every new Vote that is created or deleted.
- When a vote is changed, the signal will trigger a function that recalculates the total upvotes for the author of the commented-on post.
- If the author's total upvote count reaches 10 (or more), a Moderator object is automatically created for them. 
- If their vote count drops below 10 (e.g., votes are retracted), their Moderator status is revoked.
- The user's status will be visibly updated in the header (e.g., "Logged in as chef (Moderator)").
### Files to Create/Modify for this Task:
- comment_system/settings.py **(Done for you)**
   - Add the upvote threshold. Crucially, you must now change the INSTALLED_APPS entry from the simple 'comments' to the full AppConfig path. This is required for Django to load the signals defined in apps.py.
- comments/models.py 
   - Add the Moderator model. 
   - We also add a convenient helper function and attach it to the User model dynamically, so you can check `user.is_moderator` anywhere.
- comments/signals.py **(Done for you)**
   - Create this new file.
   - It defines functions that will be executed automatically after a Vote object is saved or deleted.
- comments/apps.py 
   - Modify this existing file to tell Django it needs to load and register the signals from signals.py when the application starts.
- comments/context_processors.py **(Done for you)**
   - Create this new file. 
   - A context processor is a function that adds variables to the context of every template that is rendered. This is the official way to make a variable like `is_moderator` available globally.
- comments/templates/comments/base.html **(Done for you)**
   - Update the header to use the new `is_moderator` context variable. 
   - Note: we could also use `user.is_moderator` because of the dynamic property we added, but using the context processor variable is also a good practice to demonstrate.

**After completing the above**
- Click on **RUN** and open the browser on a new tab.
- Create a new user, let's call them "FutureMod".
- Have "FutureMod" post one or more comments.
- Log in as another user (e.g., chef).
- Find the comments posted by "FutureMod" and give them a total of 10 or more upvotes.
- Log out and log back in as "FutureMod". You should now see "(Moderator)" next to their username in the header.
- As chef, retract one of the upvotes so the total drops to 9.
- Log in as "FutureMod" again. The "(Moderator)" status should be gone.
